data.title: Bitcoin, Ether Extend Losses as Investors Brace for Prolonged Tariff Impact
data.description:
Bitcoin and Ether fell sharply during early Asian hours on Wednesday as fears of a prolonged US-China trade war deepened, with investors bracing for President Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs to take effect.

Crypto markets, like equities, reacted to mounting uncertainty, with any hopes of a near-term tariff concession fading fast ahead of a looming deadline.

Bitcoin dropped 5.6% to $75,523 while Ether slid 10.7% to $1,417. The broader cryptocurrency market also came under pressure, with total market capitalization falling 7.2% to $2.4t in the last 24 hours, despite scattered gains in some altcoins.

Investor confidence took another hit after the . If Beijing refuses, Chinese exports will face a 104% import duty.

🇺🇸🇨🇳 The tariff situation showed a brief period of brightness today, as Bitcoin reached $80.2K again and the S&P 500 started up on the day by ~+4%. However, no notable positive progress has been announced on tariff negotiations. Developments of note:📌 Bitcoin is back down to… — Santiment (@santimentfeed) Tariff Escalation Hits Stocks as Crypto Tracks Broader Market Moves

The tariff tensions rattled traditional markets as well. All three major US stock indices ended Tuesday in the red, with the S&P 500 closing below 5,000 for the first time in nearly a year.

“With Trump threatening a further 50% tariff on China and Beijing refusing to back down, sentiment could turn quickly,” Lukman Otunuga, senior market analyst at FXTM, said. “Markets are walking a tightrope — any escalation could see risk aversion return with full force.”

Since Trump unveiled the global tariff framework last Wednesday, S&P 500 companies have lost a staggering $5.8t in market value, marking the index’s worst four-day drop since its inception in the 1950s, according to LSEG data.

Trump’s shifting rhetoric has added to the volatility. He has described the new tariffs as “permanent,” yet simultaneously claims they are forcing leaders to seek negotiations. This mixed messaging has fueled uncertainty across global markets, leaving investors unsure of what comes next.

While cryptocurrencies are often seen as uncorrelated to traditional financial systems, recent trends suggest otherwise. In times of broad market stress, Bitcoin and Ether have increasingly mirrored the movement of tech stocks and broader indices. This correlation is particularly evident during risk-off periods when investors retreat from volatile assets across the board.Inflation Report Adds to High-Stakes Week for Markets Under Pressure

Market participants are now turning their attention to Thursday’s US Consumer Price Index (CPI) report, which could further shape risk sentiment.

“A CPI reading above 3.4% could compound market concerns about persistent inflation,” said Marcin Kazmierczak, co-founder and COO of RedStone. Such an outcome may trigger another wave of equity and crypto selloffs as traders scale back expectations for near-term rate cuts, he added.

Conversely, a softer CPI reading, below 3.2%, could provide a brief reprieve, according to Kazmierczak. It might dampen inflation fears and cushion the blow from trade tensions, potentially lifting both equities and crypto.

Bitcoin, which held up better than Ether in recent days, could benefit from this scenario, bolstering views that its market behavior is beginning to diverge from traditional risk assets during episodes of macro uncertainty.

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: U.S. Tariffs Take Effect
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

No major economic updates expected; trading updates for Bang & Olufsen, JD Sports Fashion

Opening Call:

European stock futures were lower after Asia stocks fell. The dollar weakened and Treasury yields rose. Gold rose and oil fell.

Equities:

European futures were lower, as U.S. President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs took effect past midnight Eastern time and tariff turmoil continued to rattle markets, with a choppy trading session seen in Asia. Overnight, U.S. stocks ended in a downswing, capping a volatile session.

"I don't think we've seen the bottoms yet," said Donald Calcagni, chief investment officer at Mercer Advisors. "We are still early innings on this whole tariff issue, and it's going to take some time to play out."

Financial markets continue to be driven by the trade war headlines, said Ian Lyngen, rates strategist at BMO Capital Markets, adding that the 104% tariff on Chinese imports defined Tuesday's trading session on Wall Street.

Forex:

The U.S. dollar weakened, with market volatility seen likely to persist.

"We're still in the middle of it," said Marc Chandler, chief market strategist at Bannockburn Global Forex. "I think many people thought, including U.S. officials, that tariffs would be good for the dollar, but it's very choppy," he said.

The dollar has not benefited from the flight-to-safety trade it might have had in the past when markets were in turmoil. The dollar and bond yields reflect the market worrying more about growth slowing than inflation rising, Chandler added.

Bonds:

The 10-year Treasury yield continued its surprising rebound, surpassing 4.4%, with the 30-year yield jumping above 4.8%.

"Tariffs are inflationary," said Joachim Klement, investment strategist for Panmure Liberum in London.

In the immediate panic after President Trump's April 2 announcement, investors forgot that, and rushed to buy bonds at higher and higher prices. But that "makes no sense," he added.

Higher inflation should be bad for bonds, because it will erode the real value of future bond interest-rate payments. The fall in bonds since the weekend is more rational, Klement adds.

Energy:

Oil futures declined on worries over U.S. tariffs impact on oil demand.

President Trump's "tariffs have raised concerns about escalating trade tensions, particularly with China," said Hassan Fawaz, chairman and founder of GivTrade.

Metals:

Prices of gold can get to $3,400/oz given the fear and uncertainty in global markets, triggered in big part by President Trump's approach to tariffs, said MKS Pamp.

It was previously tipping a high of $3,200. Still, the precious metal--viewed by some investors as a safe haven in times of turmoil--is likely to spend more time around or below $3,000 than up around that mark, said MKS's head of research and metals strategy Nicky Shiels.

"The current 2025 floor of $2,600 will hold and unlikely to be revisited unless there large scale monetization of gold from entrenched holders like central banks," Shiels adds.

-

Copper was lower, with the base metal experiencing heavy losses since Trump announced his reciprocal tariffs on major trading partners, said ANZ research analysts.

However, prices may be supported by signs of China buying, with some keen to secure the metal at lower prices, the analysts said.

-

Iron ore prices were lower amid uncertainties on tariffs.

The fundamentals of iron ore has been relatively healthy recently, CITIC Futures analysts wrote.

owever, uncertainties surrounding the macro economy is expected to add further pressure to prices and increase the volatility of the iron ore market, they said.

In the short term, both supply and demand are solid, while the supply side may face some pressure on government restrictions of production volume, they added.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

U.S.-China Brawl Takes Center Stage in Global Trade War

SINGAPORE-Beijing threatened to "fight to the end" as President Trump was set early Wednesday morning to bring the total of the tariffs imposed on China in his second term to 104%, with both countries digging in for a protracted fight that lays bare the global economic fault line at the heart of the trade war.

While Trump has dramatically expanded his broadsides over trade to encompass allies and adversaries alike, China remains his foremost target. That reflects not just the intensifying geopolitical rivalry but also China's 20 years of growing trade surpluses with the U.S. that Trump has said have been ruinous for American jobs and industries.

Trump's Tariffs Put Fed Chair Powell in a 'No-Win Situation'

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is facing an increasingly dreadful task.

Economists, business owners and investors are betting that the uncertainty created by the sudden rollout of President Trump's large tariff hikes, many of which are set to take effect Wednesday, will push the economy closer to a recession by weakening hiring and spending. That would call for cutting rates to cushion any downturn.

Fed's Daly: Don't Expect Tariff-Induced Rate Cuts

As markets reel from renewed tariff threats and investors double down on bets for Federal Reserve rate cuts, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly is urging caution.

Speaking Tuesday at the Brigham Young University Marriott School of Business, Daly said the central bank is in a strong position to hold steady and avoid overreacting to what she called "economic uncertainty" tied to President Donald Trump's proposals for trade and fiscal policy.

Impact of Tariff Rises on U.K. Inflation Unclear, Says BOE's Lombardelli

Higher U.S. tariffs on imports from other countries will weaken U.K. economic activity, but the impact on inflation is less clear, Bank of England Deputy Governor Clare Lombardelli said Tuesday.

The BOE left its key interest rate unchanged in March, but investors expect policymakers to lower borrowing costs when they next meet in May.

Tensions With U.S. Underline Need for Digital Euro, Says ECB's Cipollone

Growing tensions between the U.S. and the European Union over trade and security underline the need for the eurozone to develop and adopt a digital euro, a senior official at the European Central Bank told lawmakers Tuesday.

The EU has been troubled by President Trump's imposition of tariffs on imports from the bloc and what it perceives as a weakening of the trans-Atlantic military alliance.

Big Bank CEOs Reckon With Their Lack of Influence on Trump

Last week, bank CEOs including JPMorgan Chase's Jamie Dimon, Goldman Sachs's David Solomon, Bank of America's Brian Moynihan and Wells Fargo's Charlie Scharf were in Washington for an industry meeting when the matter of communications with the White House came up.

Executives in the room took turns saying when they last spoke to President Trump. The general response: not recently. Many of them said they hadn't had a substantive discussion with Trump since the pandemic hammered markets in 2020, according to people familiar with the meeting.

Apple Stock Is Dropping. Trump Wants iPhones to Be Built in U.S.

President Donald Trump is serious about wanting Apple to manufacture iPhones in the U.S.

On Tuesday, when asked by a reporter if Trump wants iPhones to be made domestically, Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt replied: "Absolutely. He believes we have the labor, we have the workforce, we have the resources to do it."

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Wednesday

06:00/DEN: Feb External trade (provisional figures)

06:00/DEN: Feb Balance of payments (provisional figures)

06:00/ROM: Feb International trade

06:00/NOR: Mar PPI

07:00/SVK: Feb Foreign trade

09:00/CRO: Feb Foreign Trade

09:00/MLT: Feb International Trade

09:00/CYP: Feb Foreign Trade (provisional)

10:00/POR: Feb International trade statistics

23:01/UK: Mar RICS Residential Market Survey

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Bond rout starting to sound market alarm bells
data.description:
U.S. Treasuries extended heavy losses on Wednesday in a sign investors are selling even their safest assets as a global market rout unleashed by U.S. tariffs takes an unnerving turn towards distress and a dash for the safety of cash.

"This is beyond fundamentals right now. This is about liquidity," said Jack Chambers, senior rates strategist at ANZ in Sydney.

The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield , the globe's benchmark safe-haven anchor, was up 20 basis points and rising in Asia - a remarkable move in a time zone where it's usually fairly steady.

At 4.46% the yield is up 59 basis points from Monday's low, with traders saying hedge funds were the heaviest sellers as they started to be forced from leveraged bets that in calmer times profit from small gaps between cash and futures prices.

"This kind of thing becomes problematic if the prime broker starts saying that now ... I want to charge you a higher margin or I basically want more margins from you," said Mukesh Dave, chief investment officer at Aravali Asset Management, a global arbitrage fund based in Singapore.

Thirty-year U.S. yields (US30YT=RR) spiked 24 bps to 4.9553% and the three-day move in yield - if sustained - would mark the heaviest selloff in the long end since 1981.

Selling was heavy in Japan and Australia.

data.title: hGears Chairman to Step Down in June, Successor Named
data.description:
hGears Chairman Volker Stauch plans to step down from the supervisory board at the end of the company's annual general meeting on June 11 for personal reasons.

The precision parts company said Tuesday that Marco Freiherr von Maltzan will stand for election to succeed WKW. The appointee currently serves as supervisory board chairman at WKW.

Bitget Token (BGB) Burn Model Updated with First Quarterly Burn Exceeding 30 Million Tokens
data.description:
VICTORIA, Seychelles, April 09, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Bitget, the leading cryptocurrency exchange and Web3 company, has announced a significant update to the burn mechanism of Bitget Token (BGB). This enhancement introduces a utility-based model that ties BGB's quarterly burn amount to its on-chain usage, signifying the token's evolution towards higher transparency, compliance, and sustainable token value.

To better reflect the growing integration of BGB across centralized and decentralized ecosystems, the new burn mechanism links quarterly burn volumes to the amount of BGB used for on-chain gas fees through Bitget Wallet’s GetGas accounts. By anchoring the burn to real usage, the model facilitates BGB’s transformation as a key asset within Web3 and real-world applications. The burn formula accounts for BGB's usage as gas fees, quarterly average price, and predefined constants to ensure a dynamic and verifiable process.

The first quarterly burn under this new mechanism has now been calculated. In Q1 2025, 6,943.63 BGB were topped up in Bitget Wallet’s GetGas accounts for on-chain gas fee usage. Based on the new formula, a total of 30,006,905 BGB will be burnt in this quarter. All data related to the burn — including transaction records and wallet addresses — are publicly accessible on-chain to ensure full transparency.

“BGB is becoming a vital bridge between centralized and decentralized ecosystems. By linking its burn mechanism to actual on-chain utility, BGB's quarterly burn amount can evolve with real usage. This update incentivizes adoption and enables transparent and sustainable tokenomics,” said Gracy Chen, CEO of Bitget. “As BGB continues to expand its role in on-chain ecosystems, a more sustainable burn mechanism can be expected.”

Bitget Token (BGB) is the utility token that fuels the entire Bitget ecosystem, spanning both its centralized exchange and decentralized wallet. BGB can be staked to earn passive income or qualify for popular token airdrops via Launchpool and PoolX. It also unlocks early access to high-potential Web3 projects through Launchpad and LaunchX. On-chain, BGB is used to cover multi-chain gas fees in Bitget Wallet. Holding BGB grants users exclusive perks such as VIP-level upgrades and profit-sharing opportunities for elite traders. More than just a token, BGB is a gateway for users to engage with, influence, and grow alongside the Bitget ecosystem.

Earlier this year, the BGB ecosystem was strengthened by permanently burning 800 million team-held tokens, representing 40% of the total supply. Following this burn in January 2025, the total supply was reduced to 1.2 billion, with 100% now in circulation.

Launched in July 2021 at an initial price of 0.0585 USDT, BGB reached an all-time high of 8.5 USDT in December 2024 — delivering over 100x in cumulative gains. According to CoinMarketCap, it now ranks among the top three CEX native tokens by market cap and is listed as a top 30 crypto asset.

For more information about the BGB burn, visit this link.

About Bitget

Established in 2018, Bitget is the world's leading cryptocurrency exchange and Web3 company. Serving over 100 million users in 150+ countries and regions, the Bitget exchange is committed to helping users trade smarter with its pioneering copy trading feature and other trading solutions while offering real-time access to Bitcoin price, Ethereum price, and other cryptocurrency prices. Formerly known as BitKeep, Bitget Wallet is a world-class multi-chain crypto wallet that offers an array of comprehensive Web3 solutions and features including wallet functionality, token swap, NFT Marketplace, DApp browser, and more.

Bitget is at the forefront of driving crypto adoption through strategic partnerships, such as its role as the Official Crypto Partner of the World's Top Football League, LALIGA, in EASTERN, SEA and LATAM markets, as well as a global partner of Turkish National athletes Buse Tosun Çavuşoğlu (Wrestling world champion), Samet Gümüş (Boxing gold medalist) and İlkin Aydın (Volleyball national team), to inspire the global community to embrace the future of cryptocurrency.

For more information, visit: Website | Twitter | Telegram | LinkedIn | Discord | Bitget Wallet

For media inquiries, please contact: media@bitget.com

Risk Warning: Digital asset prices are subject to fluctuation and may experience significant volatility. Investors are advised to only allocate funds they can afford to lose. The value of any investment may be impacted, and there is a possibility that financial objectives may not be met, nor the principal investment recovered. Independent financial advice should always be sought, and personal financial experience and standing carefully considered. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future results. Bitget accepts no liability for any potential losses incurred. Nothing contained herein should be construed as financial advice. For further information, please refer to our Terms of Use.

A photo accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/081df246-71da-4680-a026-fe10467ba259

Allcargo Logistics Incorporates Step-Down Subsidiary in Turkey; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
Allcargo Logistics has incorporated a step-down subsidiary in Turkey named ECU ESC Turkey Lojistik Hizmet Merkezi Anonim Sirketi, according to a Tuesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Asiapac Turkey Tasimacilik Anonim Sirketi, a step-down subsidiary of the company, holds a 100% shareholding in the new entity.

Shares of the company were down 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Mobile Accessories Company Strax to File for Bankruptcy in Sweden
data.description:
Strax said Tuesday its directors resolved to apply for bankruptcy with the Stockholm District Court in Sweden.

The Swedish mobile accessories company said it made significant efforts to resolve its financing troubles, but its issues eventually became "too extensive to overcome."

Strax said its challenges started during the COVID-19 pandemic and were exacerbated by higher interest costs and a dispute with the majority shareholder of a German distribution platform.

data.title: Zee Media Gets Board Nod to Raise $47 Million via Foreign Currency Convertible Bonds
data.description:
Zee Media said its board approved raising up to $46.6 million through issuance of unsecured, unlisted foreign currency convertible bonds, according to an Indian bourse filing on Tuesday.

The bonds carry a 5% coupon and have a 10-year maturity, and will be issued on a private placement basis to UNICO Global Opportunities Fund and Sun India Opportunities Investing Fund, the filing said.

The issuance opened on Tuesday. The bonds are optionally convertible into equity shares at 13.50 Indian rupees per share, translating to 296,135,358 shares on a fully diluted basis, the filing added.

data.title: Fitch Affirms ERG Ratings on Visible Cash Flow Generation
data.description:
Fitch Ratings affirmed on Tuesday the long-term issuer default and senior unsecured ratings of ERG (ERG.MI) at BBB-, citing the company's visible cash flow generation.

The rating agency said the cash flow is expected from the Italian renewables generation company's primarily long-term incentivized and contracted clean onshore energy production.

The stable outlook reflects Fitch's forecast for average funds from operations net leverage of 4.3x for the years 2025 through 2027. Fitch also highlighted ERG's commitment to an investment-grade rating.

data.title: Foxconn wants to work with Nissan on EVs, strategy chief tells Nikkei
data.description:
Foxconn wants to cooperate with Nissan but is currently not engaging with the Japanese automaker, an executive in charge of the Taiwan company's electric vehicle business told the Nikkei newspaper in an interview published on Wednesday.

Foxconn Chief Strategy Officer for EVs Jun Seki made the remarks ahead of a seminar in Tokyo on Wednesday where the contract manufacturer aims to lay out its EV strategy to Japanese companies.

"From our perspective, the compatibility with Japanese automakers, especially Nissan, will become a strength" for Foxconn, Nikkei cited Seki, a former senior Nissan executive, as saying.

Seki also said it was a great idea for Foxconn to be part of a potential broader partnership with Honda , Nissan and Mitsubishi Motors and confirmed that Foxconn was finalising a deal with Mitsubishi to supply it with EVs.

Sources had previously told Reuters that Nissan's junior partner Mitsubishi plans to outsource the production of EVs for Oceania markets to Foxconn, which also makes iPhones for Apple and artificial intelligence servers for Nvidia .

In February, Nikkei reported that Foxconn had proposed partnering with Honda with an ultimate aim of creating a four-way framework that would also include Nissan and Mitsubishi after Honda-Nissan merger talks collapsed.

Foxconn has previously said it would consider taking a stake in Nissan, but that its main aim was cooperation.

data.title: Market Chatter: Korea International Trade Association Urges US to Exempt Korean Copper from Potential Tariffs
data.description:
The Korea International Trade Association (KITA) voiced opposition to potential US tariffs on copper imports, arguing that Korean products do not pose a national security risk, Yonhap News reported Tuesday.

KITA, in a letter to the US Commerce Department, emphasized South Korea's 3.5% share in US copper imports and highlighted key investments by LS Cable & System and Poongsan Corporation .

LS Cable & System is set to break ground later in April on a new submarine power cable facility in Virginia, backed by an $800 million investment. Meanwhile, Poongsan has maintained a strong US presence since 1992 with its Iowa plant, turning out 54,000 tons of copper and alloy products a year, the report said.

KITA called for either a full exemption or a narrowed tariff scope to avoid harm to downstream US industries, according to the report.

Shares of Poongsan Corporation fell more than 1% in recent trade on Wednesday.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Wednesday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Big Bank CEOs Reckon With Their Lack of Influence on Trump

Bankers are uncertain about Trump's endgame on tariffs and uneasy about the impact on the global economy and their own businesses.

Janus Henderson Strikes Deal to Manage $45 Billion of Insurer's Assets

Guardian Life will seed new initiatives and receive warrants to buy Janus stock.

Stocks Are in Turmoil, but Treasury Yields Are Stubbornly High

Bond traders appear to be betting on a return to normal before long.

Pantheon Raises $5.2 Billion for Private Credit Secondaries

The firm expects to capitalize on ripe opportunities as liquidity needs rise, especially after recent market volatility.

Barings Makes $200 Million Bet on Air Passenger Demand

The Charlotte, N.C., investment manager is buying 12 older aircraft as demand for new vessels far outstrips supply.

Excelsior Energy Expands Scope With $1 Billion Second Fund

The midmarket renewable-energy infrastructure fund will expand beyond later-stage financing to consider projects at slightly earlier stages of development.

CEOs Break Silence on Trump Trade War

For weeks, business leaders have avoided voicing concerns about tariffs. Some executives are getting more vocal.

Belluscura Scraps Market Outlook Due to Tariff Uncertainties

The medical equipment developer said it is assessing the potential financial implications, particularly from the 54% tariff on Chinese goods due to take effect this week.

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Read about American Express, Levi Strauss, Prudential PLC, and more in the latest Market Talks covering Financial Services.

Wall Street Starts to Speak Out Against Trump's Tariffs

Stan Druckenmiller, Bill Ackman and Jamie Dimon are among those raising concerns about the president's steep, across-the-globe levies.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Wednesday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Oil Futures Fall Further on Demand Concerns

Crude oil futures gave up early gains and fell for a fourth straight session as continuing trade wars add to concerns about the loss of demand. WTI settled down 1.8% at $59.58 a barrel, and Brent fell 2.2% to $62.82 a barrel.

The Stock Market Is Bad. The Oil Market Might Be Worse.

Oil prices are down 15% for the year, including a 2.1% fall on Monday.

Shell Lowers Gas Production Guidance

The energy giant lowered its integrated gas production estimate for the first quarter as volumes were hit by unplanned maintenance.

Trump Promised to Lower Energy Prices-but It Wasn't Supposed to Be Like This

The president's tariff blitz sent oil prices tumbling 14% in two days, bludgeoning frackers.

Woodside to Sell 40% Stake in Louisiana LNG Infrastructure

Stonepeak has agreed to invest $5.7 billion in Woodside Energy's proposed Louisiana LNG gas-export infrastructure in the U.S.

Brookfield Buys Colonial Enterprises for $9 Billion

The equity investment in the oil-pipeline firm will be around $500 million, Brookfield said. The agreement includes buying Shell's 16.125% stake in the business.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Caterpillar, Shell, OMV and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

Activist Elliott Names Nominees for Phillips 66 Board Amid Proxy Fight

Activist investor Elliott Investment Management disclosed the names of its four nominees to join Phillips 66' board of directors, in a move that comes shortly after it sued the oil refiner and its board for seat changes.

BP Chairman Helge Lund to Step Down Amid Push for Changes at Oil Major

The British energy company said Lund is likely to step down sometime next year, as activist investor Elliott Management pushes for changes at the British oil major.

OPEC+ to Make Larger Output Hike in May, Oil Tumbles

Eight OPEC+ countries will increase output by 411,000 barrels a day next month, prompting oil prices to fall further.

data.title: Higher U.S. Rice Imports Could Be Japan's Negotiating Card With TrumpMarket Talk
data.description: Importing more rice from the U.S. could be one of Japan's negotiating cards as it seeks exemption from President Trump's higher tariffs, says Mizuho Securities economist Yasunari Ueno. "A rise in rice prices has become a major political issue in Japan, and the prevailing view is that the cause is a tight supply-demand balance," he says. Importing rice from the U.S. could help ease pressure on Japanese rice prices. "If the selling prices drop too much, it could hit domestic farmers, but in that case, the government could provide support," he adds. The Trump administration has said Japan charges a 700% tariff on rice imported from the U.S, which Japan says isn't true. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0350 GMT - Palm oil plantation companies with higher Malaysia exposure could fare better amid uncertainties in Indonesia, CGS International's Jacquelyn Yow says in a note. Indonesia may seize over one million hectares of private oil palm plantations deemed illegal, in a bid to clean up the industry's image and boost state revenue, she notes. Indonesia's potential market share loss to Malaysia due to higher U.S. tariffs and rising operational costs from a weakening rupiah are other headwinds, she says. CGS International keeps a neutral rating on Malaysia's agribusiness sector and pegs SD Guthrie and Hap Seng Plantations as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0319 GMT - Shares of BHP have outperformed those in Anglo American so far this year, leading Jefferies analysts to wonder: Could a takeover be back on the table in 2025? BHP unsuccessfully tried to buy its rival a year ago, but the rationale for a deal--its relentless pursuit of copper--still stands, the analysts say. They reckon Anglo is "arguably on the cusp of becoming a more compelling target," given a significant part of its own restructuring should soon be done. "An argument against a move on Anglo could be risk of near-term earnings dilution, but BHP may be willing to do a deal that is perceived to be dilutive in the short term to become a bigger copper miner and better company for the long term," the analysts say. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0311 GMT - Iron ore prices are lower in early Asia trade amid uncertainties on tariffs. The fundamentals of iron ore has been relatively healthy recently, CITIC Futures analysts write in a note. However, uncertainties surrounding the macro economy further pressure prices and increase the volatility of the iron ore market, they say. In the short term, both supply and demand are solid, while the supply side may face some pressure on government restrictions of production volume, they add. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is down 3.0% at CNY687.50 a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0250 GMT - Palm oil prices fall in Asian trading, tracking soybean oil's weakness overnight on the Chicago Board of Trade and lower palm olein prices on the Dalian Commodity Exchange, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. U.S. President Trump's 104% tariff on Chinese imports is set to take effect, raising recession fears and threatening global commodity demand, it adds. AmInvestment Bank sees support for crude palm oil prices at 4,146 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,255 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for June delivery is down 10 ringgit at 4,178 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0250 GMT - Gold is higher, reversing earlier losses, on likely safe-haven demand for the precious metal. There are "fears that an increasing hostile trade war will cause an economic downturn," ANZ Research analysts say, noting the U.S.' plan to impose 104% tariffs on imports from China. Gold was lower earlier in the session on possible unwinding of long positions to raise cash. Spot gold is 0.8% higher at $3,005.48/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0242 GMT - MKS Pamp reckons the gold price can get to $3,400/oz given the fear and uncertainty in global markets, triggered in big part by President Trump's approach to tariffs. It was previously tipping a high of $3,200. Still, the precious metal--viewed by some investors as a safe haven in times of turmoil--is likely to spend more time around or below $3,000 than up around that mark, according to MKS's head of research and metals strategy Nicky Shiels. "The current 2025 floor of $2,600 will hold and unlikely to be revisited unless there large scale monetization of gold from entrenched holders like central banks," Shiels adds. Spot gold is up 0.8% at $3,005.63/oz. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0229 GMT - Copper is lower in early Asian trade. The base metal has experienced heavy losses since Trump announced his reciprocal tariffs on major trading partners, according to ANZ research analysts in a commentary. However, prices may be supported by signs of China buying, with some keen to secure the metal at lower prices, the analysts say. The three-month LME copper contract is 0.6% lower at $8,601.00 a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0200 GMT - Australian miners are deep in the red on President Trump's soon-to-be-enacted 104% tariff on imports from China, the dominant buyer in global commodity markets. BHP and Rio Tinto, the world's two biggest miners by market value, are down 3.0% and 4.2%, respectively. Investors fear the escalating trade war between the world's largest economies will weigh on commodity demand. Australia's metals and mining index is 3.1% lower. Lithium miners face a double whammy after President Trump signed executive orders aimed at revamping the U.S. coal industry and increasing the use of the fuel to generate electricity. Part of the reason some investors are bullish on lithium--a key battery ingredient--is the expectation of massive growth in energy storage for renewable-power generation. Producer Pilbara Minerals is down 6.0%. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2343 GMT - BMI expects more weakness in lithium prices ahead as "unwaveringly resilient lithium supply continues to saturate the market." BMI, a unit of Fitch Solutions, forecasts mainland Chinese lithium carbonate will average $9,500/metric ton in 2025, versus a year to date price of $10,313/ton. It predicts an average of $9,000/ton for Chinese lithium hydroxide monohydrate, versus $9,619/ton year to date. "Although the lithium market was buoyed by production cuts announcements late last year in response to plummeting prices, persistent oversupply concerns, driven by capacity restarts and cost-cutting measures, have reignited bearish sentiment," says BMI. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2342 GMT - Gold falls in the early Asian session on possible unwinding of long positions to raise cash. "This may sound strange, but it has happened several times in periods of heightened risk aversion, as market participants have to sell gold positions to offset losses elsewhere," Commerzbank Research's Carsten Fritsch says in a research report. However, the recent increase in Fed rate-cut expectations suggests that gold price will likely soon rise again, the commodity analyst adds, noting Fed funds futures imply 100bps worth of rate cuts by the year-end. Spot gold is down 0.3% to $2,974.24/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1927 GMT - Live cattle and lean hog futures on the CME finish lower after starting the day higher — with traders sliding into being risk-off on livestock even as other segments like grains and oilseeds gained on implications that tariffs coming into force before many U.S. farmers start planting will change their outlook for how many acres they plant. For livestock, cutting exposure to risk was still a chief focus. "[Livestock] is trading uncertainty as much as anything and trying to minimize risk," says Karl Setzer of Consus Ag Consulting in a note. Live cattle finish down 0.2% to $1.94375 a pound, while hogs fell 0.2% to 90.825 cents a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1920 GMT - Crude oil futures give up early gains and fall for a fourth straight session as continuing trade wars add to concerns about loss of demand. "Unfortunately for crude oil, tariff demand destruction is only half of the problem," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note, pointing the OPEC+ decision to add more than 400,000 barrels a day to output next month on top of this month's 138,000 barrels a day increase. OPEC and the IEA are likely to cut their demand estimates in next week's monthly reports, he adds. "It will be interesting to see how OPEC justifies increasing May production by a total of 538,000 barrels a day." WTI settles down 1.8% at $59.58 a barrel, and Brent falls 2.2% to $62.82 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: BHP Target Price Cut 4.7% to A$45.10/Share by Goldman Sachs>BHP.AU
Japan 10-Year Yield Tracks Treasury Yields Higher
data.description:
Japan’s 10-year government bond yield surged above 1.3% on Wednesday, rebounding further from three-month lows and tracking a rally in US Treasury yields.

Those moves came on speculation that foreign investors are dumping Treasuries due to trade-related policy concerns.

US President Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs came into effect today, including a cumulative 104% levy on Chinese imports.

Japan also faces a 24% tariff on its goods and a 25% levy on car shipments to the US. Meanwhile, Trump said that Japan is sending a team to negotiate on trade with the US, while Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba revealed that he told Trump in a phone call to rethink his tariff policies.

data.title: Indian bond yields track US Treasuries higher; traders bet on dovish RBI policy
data.description:
Indian government bond yield witnessed an upswing in early deals on Wednesday, with the benchmark yield surpassing the 6.50% handle, tracking a steep spike in U.S. Treasury yields.

However, investors remained optimistic about a dovish decision and guidance from the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), which will announce its policy at 10:00 a.m. IST.

The benchmark 10-year bond yield (IN067934G=CC) was 6.5125% as of 9:45 a.m. IST, compared to Tuesday's close of 6.4747%.

"Had it not been for the RBI policy, we could easily have reacted 5-7 basis points (bps) more to such moves in Treasuries," a trader with a private bank said.

The RBI is set to deliver a second rate cut this year on the day amid growth risks from the sweeping U.S. import tariffs.

Investors have already factored in a 25 bps cut, while the overnight indexed swap (OIS) rates have fallen so far in April, signalling that the RBI could also change its stance.

Bets have risen for an aggregate rate cut of 100 bps from 75 bps earlier. The RBI began its easing cycle in February after a nearly five-year break.

Liquidity guidance will be in focus, especially as bankers seek comfort through the availability of overnight liquidity.

The RBI has infused over 5.4 trillion rupees ($62.41 billion) into the banking system since the start of 2025 through debt purchases and foreign exchange swaps and will buy bonds worth 400 billion rupees in April.

IDFC First Bank anticipates a significant liquidity surplus between 0.5% and 1.4% of deposits in the April-June period.

It also sees a durable liquidity infusion of 3 trillion-4 trillion rupees to maintain a 1% system liquidity surplus.

($1 = 86.5300 Indian rupees)

data.title: Trump Reciprocal Tariffs Take Effect, Upending U.S. Trade
data.description:
By Gavin Bade

President Trump imposed more tariffs on nearly 100 nations, hiking U.S. duties to the highest level since before World War II and sparking a race among many targeted nations to renegotiate terms of trade with the U.S. The levies were part of what Trump has called his reciprocal tariff regime, taking aim at nations his administration considers bad actors on trade.

Many southeast Asian nations were hardest hit, with Vietnam, Laos and Cambodia receiving tariff hikes of over 45%. China will receive an 84% tariff hike, putting Trump's tariffs on all Chinese imports at 104%. Including levies on certain products from before Trump's second term, the average tariff rate on China is estimated at around 125%. Trump's team has said that countries may be able to negotiate lower tariffs by decreasing their own duties and ending non-tariff barriers on U.S. goods, but no deals have yet been reached.

In many cases, those so-called reciprocal tariff amounts appear to match a basic formula: the size of a country's goods-trade imbalance with the U.S., divided by how much America imports from that nation.

data.title: Nikkei Stock Average Extends Losses, Now Down 4.1% at 31656.42
SES Receives New Contracts from Uzbek, Thai Airlines
data.description:
Satellite operator and data connectivity services provider SES said Tuesday it received two new contracts from Uzbekistan Airways and Thai Airways.

The company will provide its SES Open Orbits global inflight connectivity network for both airlines. The systems will be integrated in Uzbekistan Airways' upcoming A321neo aircraft and Thai Airways' future B787 aircraft in 2026.
